text: Hello, sharing a robotic tap umbilical hernia repair. It's actually an incisional hernia. The patient had a previous cholecystectomy and hernia is at the umbilical port site. Tap has become my go-to for this as I try to keep mesh out of the abdomen.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the thin peritoneum, avoiding excessive tension to prevent damage to the ipsilateral flap, while gradually working down the midline, considering the amount of visceral preperitoneal fat present.

The surgeon begins the procedure approximately five centimeters to the ipsilateral side of the hernia defect, using a fifteen by fifteen mesh. The flap is extended farther to the contralateral side to reduce tension and ensure the mesh covers the defect adequately.

The surgeon bluntly dissects through the preperitoneal fat plane, which separates easily, revealing the area around the umbilicus where the incisional hernia is located. The tissue appears more scarred than usual due to the previous surgical incision.

The surgeon carefully performs sharp dissection to reduce the incisional hernia, making an effort to keep the peritoneal sac intact and avoid creating holes that could increase tension during closure.

The surgeon dissects above and below the hernia, crossing the midline to approach the hernia directly. The dissection reveals the thin peritoneum on the contralateral side, outside the repartineal midline fat.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the posterior sheath and transversalis fascia, avoiding grabbing with the left hand to prevent tearing the thin tissue. The dissection is performed gently, pushing down rather than pulling, to minimize the risk of creating holes in the delicate structures.
The surgeon prepares to reduce the hernia sac, carefully attempting to extract as much peritoneum from the defect as possible while avoiding the creation of holes.

The surgeon continues the dissection on the opposite side of the hernia, attempting to recruit additional peritoneum despite the presence of holes, to ensure a more redundant tissue closure of the peritoneal defect.
These cases take me about one hour. Like I said, I will almost always use a fifteen by fifteen mesh. My patients do get tap blocks and nobody or almost nobody goes home with any narcotics.

The surgeon continues the dissection on the contralateral side, progressing bluntly and easily in a good plane. The peritoneal flap is gently pushed down without grabbing, aiming to create a pocket larger than fifteen by fifteen centimeters to ensure the flap can cover the mesh without tension across the ipsilateral side.
The surgeon continues dissection on the contralateral side, aiming to reduce tension and achieve redundancy of the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon sews the posterior peritoneal hole closed, ensuring the closure is perpendicular to avoid shortening the flap. Subsequently, the hernia defect is closed.
The surgeon attempts to imbricate the dermis, folding and suturing the dermal layers to reinforce the closure.

do decrease the insufflation for these closures in this case i believe i went down to eight for the smaller defects like this i don't know how much it really matters i just don't like the idea of putting these sutures in under tension that theoretically could be loose after desufflation and when the patient starts recruiting and accessing their muscles I think maybe running it all the way back like this might be a little bit overkill, but I do try to use the majority of the suture that I was given.

The surgeon places a 15x15 mesh over the hernia defect, securing it with a trio silk suture tied in the middle and positioned below the defect. Sutures are placed in all four corners to hold the mesh edges and prevent clamshelling or folding. The flap is then sewn closed.

The surgeon adjusts the camera position due to close port placement near the midline, sewing on the side while pulling the peritoneal flap to the ipsilateral side. The fifteen by fifteen mesh is positioned outside the peritoneal pocket, and the flap is pulled across to ensure the mesh remains covered by peritoneum, preventing exposure to the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon closes several sub-centimeter holes using 3-0 Vicryl sutures, completing the repair of the hernia defect.
